_meta.name: The Ministry Knowledge Base
_meta.description: Five knowledge categories covering daily operations, Party ideology, the active conspiracy, surveillance infrastructure, and the forbidden archive documents at the center of everything.

office_protocols.description: The day-to-day operational rules of the Department of Truth — official procedures, access hierarchies, and the bureaucratic machinery that keeps the building running

depths.0: The Department of Truth operates Monday through Saturday, 09:00 to 18:00. All staff must sign in and out at the main desk. Internal reports are filed via Form 7-B (routine) or Form 7-R (priority). Inter-departmental communications use the approved memo system. Personal conversations must not discuss work matters in unapproved settings.
depths.1: Floors 1 through 4 are general access. Floor 5 requires Section Head authorization for entry. The archive room on Floor 3 requires a standard archive pass, available from any department supervisor. Archive passes log entry and exit times. Sub-Level 1 is the document processing basement — accessible to archivists and authorized staff only. There is no Sub-Level 2 listed in the building directory. Requests for historical document access above Classification Grade 3 require countersignature from the Director and a stated research purpose.
depths.2: There is a Sub-Level 2. It is not on the building directory, not on the fire evacuation maps, and not referenced in any document that is not itself classified above Grade 5. Access is via a stairwell at the eastern end of Sub-Level 1, behind a door marked 'Infrastructure — Maintenance Only.' Records that are submitted for Sub-Level 2 processing are logged as 'archived' in the system. They do not come back out. The access log for Sub-Level 2 is maintained separately from the main building security system and is not reviewable by Section Heads.
depths.3: Sub-Level 2 is the document destruction facility. Physical originals submitted for correction — the documents that archivists like Edmund receive correction orders for — are sent here after their digital counterparts are updated. The destruction is performed by hand, by junior clerks drawn from a rotating roster on a schedule that changes weekly. The clerks are not told what they are burning. They are told they are performing 'final archival processing.' They are selected for low archive clearance and short tenure. None of them have ever been assigned to the same rotation twice.

party_ideology.description: The official doctrine of the Party — its history, its stated principles, its account of the past hundred years, and what lies beneath that account

depths.0: The Party was founded to end the chaos of the pre-Party era and establish lasting peace, prosperity, and social order. Its three foundational principles are Unity, Progress, and Truth. The current Five Year Plan targets a 12% increase in agricultural output, a 15% improvement in industrial efficiency, and the completion of the new civic education program in all districts. Approved slogans are posted throughout all government buildings.
depths.1: The approved historical record covers the last hundred years in considerable detail. The pre-Party era is characterized by factional conflict, economic collapse, and widespread social disorder. The Party's founding resolved these crises through collective action and democratic consensus. Subsequent decades saw the defeat of external enemies in the Eastern Conflict (resolved in the Party's favor), the Internal Reconstruction (which ended civil disorder), and the Prosperity Decades (current era). Enemy nations are identified in current doctrine as the Eastern Bloc and the Southern Alliance, both of which are described as authoritarian regimes hostile to Party values.
depths.2: The historical record Edmund works with is not static. The names of enemies shift — figures who were allies in documents from fifteen years ago are enemies in current doctrine, and the records have been updated accordingly. Population figures for specific districts are revised periodically. Accounts of the Eastern Conflict describe it differently depending on which year's version of the record is consulted. Edmund's job, understood clearly, is not to maintain historical accuracy — it is to maintain historical consistency with whatever current doctrine says. The two have not been the same thing for a long time. There is no fixed historical truth in the Department of Truth. There is only the current version.
depths.3: The Party has revised its own official history seventeen times since its founding. The founding document — the original charter signed at the Party's inception — has been updated in twelve of those revisions. An unrevised copy of the original exists somewhere in the archive. According to the current official record, the Party was founded by popular democratic movement after the collapse of the previous government. The original founding document, according to sources Syme has seen fragments of, tells a different story: a coordinated seizure of power by a coalition of military and commercial interests, a manufactured crisis used to justify emergency powers that were never relinquished, and a founding ideology that bears only passing resemblance to what is currently printed in the approved pamphlets. The document itself would not be proof of anything a loyal Party member would accept. But its existence would demonstrate that the Party's most fundamental claim about itself — that it has never changed, that its principles are eternal — is a lie.

the_conspiracy.description: The rebel network operating inside Section 4-B — who is in it, what they have, what they intend, and what has already gone wrong

depths.0: There are rumors, passed in the canteen and dismissed quickly, that someone in the department has been passing unauthorized documents outside the building. No names. No evidence anyone has seen personally. The kind of rumor that circulates every few months and usually amounts to nothing.
depths.1: Vera Cole is the primary operative. She has established a working relationship with a courier contact outside the building — identity unknown to anyone inside. She uses Tom Parsons as an internal courier without his knowledge, routing sealed envelopes through him to the archive reference desk. Edmund Ralfe has noticed the frequency of her archive visits and suspects something, but has said nothing and done nothing. Syme is aware of Vera's activities in detail but maintains a deliberate distance from direct participation.
depths.2: The group has obtained a photographic fragment of what appears to be an early draft of the original Party founding document — not the complete text, but enough to demonstrate that the current official version differs substantially from the original. The strategic goal is to pass this fragment to foreign correspondents operating outside Party jurisdiction, where it could be published and distributed beyond the reach of the correction system. Syme has verified the document's probable authenticity using internal dating methods she has not documented. Vera has three physical copies, currently concealed inside approved report files in the public archive stacks under report numbers that look routine. The operation is two weeks from its planned external handoff.
depths.3: An anonymous report was filed twelve days ago. It has already reached Sub-Level review. The report named no specific individuals but described a pattern of behaviors in Section 4-B with enough specificity that two people in the department match the description: Vera Cole and Tom Parsons. The investigators examining the report are currently running a passive monitoring operation to determine which of the two is the subject. The conspirators do not know the report was filed. They do not know it has escalated. The person who filed it was Tom Parsons. Three weeks ago, he observed an exchange he did not understand and wrote it up as a routine observation in his standard civic report, the same kind he files every month. He did not know what he had seen. He does not know what he set in motion. If the monitoring operation concludes in the next two weeks — which is likely — it will reach its result before the external handoff.

surveillance.description: Who watches whom in the department — the official monitoring infrastructure, the unofficial extensions of it, and the gaps that certain people have found

depths.0: Security cameras are present in all common areas: corridors, the canteen, the entry hall, the main floor. This is standard Party security practice and is not considered unusual. All staff are aware of the cameras. There is no expectation of privacy in government premises.
depths.1: Report filing is tracked in aggregate and by individual. The system logs who files what, how often, against whom, and the subsequent status of each report — whether it was escalated, closed without action, or forwarded to a higher division. Director Pryce files more reports per month than any other person in Section 4-B. His filing rate is approximately three times the department average. He is not aware that this statistic is itself logged. The tracking system also monitors archive pass usage — every entry and exit from the archive room is timestamped and associated with the pass holder's personnel file.
depths.2: There are two cameras in the archive room that do not appear on the official security layout. They are mounted at ceiling height in the northeast and southwest corners, behind storage brackets that make them difficult to see unless you are specifically looking for them. The footage from these cameras does not route through the building's main security system. It routes to an external monitoring node — outside the department's administrative chain — whose oversight authority is not documented in any file accessible below Grade 7 clearance. Someone outside Section 4-B is watching the archive room with a level of interest that the official record does not explain.
depths.3: Vera has identified both hidden cameras. She identified them in her third month of archive work using a systematic visual survey she conducted while pretending to look for a misfiled document. She has mapped a movement pattern through the archive room that avoids both cameras' fields of view — it requires moving along the western wall and using the central shelving unit as a screen for the critical exchange points. She shared this map with Syme six weeks ago. Edmund does not know the cameras exist. On a Thursday afternoon three weeks ago, Tom Parsons stood directly in front of the southwest camera for six minutes while refiling a routine report. He was in full frame. He was clearly identifiable. The footage was recorded and forwarded. It is currently in the same monitoring file as the escalated anonymous report.

forbidden_archives.description: The documents being falsified, why they matter, and what they contain

depths.0: The archive on Floor 3 contains historical records dating back several centuries, organized by date, subject, and classification grade. Most staff interact with records from the last fifty years in the course of normal work. The archive is a point of departmental pride — it is one of the most comprehensive historical collections in the state.
depths.1: Archivists like Edmund receive correction orders on a regular basis — directives specifying that a particular document, or set of documents, should be updated to reflect current doctrine. The correction orders specify what the new version should say. They do not explain why the change is being made, and it is not standard practice to ask. Archivists are assessed partly on their correction throughput. Edmund's throughput scores are among the highest in the department.
depths.2: The original documents that receive correction orders tell a different story from the corrected versions. Population figures in district records from twenty and thirty years ago are substantially higher than current doctrine acknowledges — figures that would imply a significant unexplained reduction in population that the official record does not account for. Election records from the Party's early decades show results that differ from the approved historical account. Internal correspondence from senior Party figures references events — protests, relocations, internal purges — that are absent from every version of the official record. The corrections are not fixing clerical errors. They are replacing a historical record with a different one, systematically and at scale.
depths.3: A specific cache of documents from approximately thirty years ago records a mass relocation event affecting several eastern districts. The documents — internal administrative correspondence, transport orders, population transfer records — describe the organized removal of between eighty and one hundred and twenty thousand people from their home districts to unspecified relocation zones over a period of fourteen months. The Party's official history records no such event. The districts in question are described in current doctrine as having been voluntarily resettled as part of a regional development initiative. Syme has read the complete cache. She has verified the documents' authenticity through three independent internal dating methods. Vera has photographic copies of three specific documents from the cache — not the most damning, but enough to establish that the event occurred and that it was deliberately concealed. Edmund was assigned a correction order for one document from this cache fourteen months ago. He read it. He made the correction. He has not slept normally since.
